questions.question: Why doesn't adding more agents fix generic multi-agent output?
options: More agents exceed rate limits
Three coders in a group chat write three flavors of the same mediocre code — the fix is DIFFERENT agents with distinct roles, tools, and grounded correction, not parallel guessing
Agents refuse to work in groups larger than three
More agents always means more hallucination
questions.explanation: Specialization creates internal disagreement with grounded correction: give the critic tools the planner lacks, give the verifier an objective test suite. Homogeneous agents just vote on shared blind spots.

questions.question: What distinguishes the critic role from the verifier role?
options: They are synonyms
The critic is subjective and LLM-based (reviews quality, can be fooled by plausible prose); the verifier is deterministic and code-based (test runner, type checker — pass/fail with evidence)
The critic runs first, so it is more important
Verifiers are only for security audits
questions.explanation: Use both: the critic catches taste issues a verifier can't articulate; the verifier catches runtime bugs the critic can't see. Collapsing them into one LLM role is the classic anti-pattern.

questions.question: What does MetaGPT's Code = SOP(Team) formulation mean?
options: Code must follow company style guides
Software-engineering SOPs are encoded as role prompts (PM → Architect → Project Manager → Engineer → QA) with strict input/output schemas, turning a team of LLMs into a predictable pipeline
Teams should be sized by lines of code
Standard operating procedures replace all code
questions.explanation: Each role prompt says what the role is and what it must produce. Determinism comes from the SOP structure, not from the models — the same insight ChatDev applies through its chat chain.

questions.question: What is ChatDev's "communicative dehallucination"?
options: A filter that deletes hallucinated tokens
When an executor needs a detail not in the plan, it explicitly asks the relevant role by name instead of plausibly inventing it
Running the same prompt twice and diffing
A fine-tuning method against hallucination
questions.explanation: The role prompt includes "when you need information you were not given, ask before producing output" — directly preventing the classic failure where an LLM confidently fabricates the missing detail.

questions.question: What evidence makes the verifier the load-bearing role?
options: Verifiers are the cheapest agents to run

MAST traced 1,642 multi-agent failures with 21.3% being verification gaps and most others tracing to silent or missing checks; PwC measured 10% → 70% accuracy (7x) from adding a structured validation loop
Verifiers write the best documentation
No evidence — it is a design preference
questions.explanation: Every multi-agent failure ultimately traces to missing or broken verification. The minimal fix for any system: at least one role whose pass/fail is decided by code, not by an LLM saying "looks good to me."
